exclusion_rules.description: TB diagnostic and treatment services apply to persons with presumptive TB (cough >2 weeks, fever, weight loss) or diagnosed TB. Anyone with symptoms can walk in for free testing — there is no income, age, or category bar. This is not a hard exclusion: the programme actively seeks out presumptive cases.

family_criteria.family_definition: Universal access — every individual in India is entitled to free TB diagnosis and treatment regardless of income, family size, or social category. Household contacts of TB patients are also screened and offered free TB Preventive Treatment (TPT).

covered_procedures: Free TB diagnosis — sputum microscopy, NAAT/CBNAAT (GeneXpert), Truenat molecular testing
Free chest X-ray for presumptive TB cases
Free drug-susceptibility testing (DST) including Line Probe Assay for drug-resistant TB
Free anti-TB drugs (first-line FDC) for drug-sensitive TB for the full 6-month course
Free second-line and newer drugs (Bedaquiline, Delamanid) for MDR-TB and XDR-TB
Daily fixed-dose combination (FDC) regimen under DOTS
Nutrition support of Rs 1000 per month via DBT for the full treatment duration (Ni-kshay Poshan Yojana)
Free TB Preventive Treatment (TPT) for household contacts and high-risk groups
Free treatment of TB-HIV co-infection in coordination with NACO
Follow-up sputum testing and treatment monitoring at no cost
Treatment adherence support via Ni-kshay Mitra and digital adherence technologies
excluded_procedures: Private hospital room charges or non-TB hospitalization costs
Treatment of unrelated illnesses (covered under other schemes such as PM-JAY)

required_documents.name: Aadhaar Card
required_documents.notes: Helpful for Ni-kshay registration and DBT nutrition support, but TB testing and treatment are NOT denied for lack of ID. Aadhaar is needed only to receive the Rs 1000/month DBT.

required_documents.name: Bank Account (Aadhaar-linked)
required_documents.notes: Required only to receive the Rs 1000/month nutrition DBT. Not required for free diagnosis or drugs.

enrollment_channels: Any government hospital, PHC, CHC, or district hospital
Designated Microscopy Centre (DMC) at government facilities
Private doctors and labs notifying through Ni-kshay portal
ASHA / ANM community health worker referral
enrollment_steps: Step 1: Visit any government health facility (PHC, CHC, district hospital, or DMC) if you have cough for more than 2 weeks, fever, night sweats, or weight loss
Step 2: Provide a sputum sample for free microscopy / NAAT (CBNAAT or Truenat) molecular testing
Step 3: Get a free chest X-ray if advised by the medical officer
Step 4: If diagnosed with TB, the treating doctor notifies (registers) your case on the Ni-kshay portal — mandatory by law
Step 5: Receive free anti-TB drugs and begin the DOTS treatment regimen at the nearest facility
Step 6: Provide Aadhaar and bank details so the Rs 1000/month nutrition DBT can be credited
Step 7: Attend follow-up visits for sputum re-testing and complete the full treatment course (6 months for drug-sensitive TB)
Step 8: Household contacts are screened and offered free TB Preventive Treatment
description_for_embedding: The National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP), formerly the Revised National TB Control Programme (RNTCP), is India's central government programme to eliminate tuberculosis under the TB Mukt Bharat mission. It provides completely FREE TB services to every person in India regardless of income, age, or social category. Free services include sputum microscopy, molecular diagnostics (CBNAAT/GeneXpert, Truenat), chest X-ray, drug-susceptibility testing, and the full course of quality-assured anti-TB drugs delivered under DOTS. Drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB, XDR-TB) patients receive free second-line and newer drugs such as Bedaquiline and Delamanid. All notified TB patients also get Rs 1000 per month as Direct Benefit Transfer for nutrition support (Ni-kshay Poshan Yojana) for the full treatment duration. Household contacts and high-risk groups are screened and offered free TB Preventive Treatment. Anyone with TB symptoms — cough over two weeks, fever, weight loss, night sweats — can walk into any government health facility (PHC, CHC, district hospital, or designated microscopy centre) for free testing and treatment. Services are delivered through the public health system and coordinated via the Ni-kshay digital portal, where private providers are also legally required to notify TB cases. NTEP is broader than the standalone Ni-kshay Poshan nutrition scheme: it covers the entire diagnosis-to-cure pathway at no cost to the patient.
